tweets.title: My two cents on rocket force debate : Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid. But I think one important question is often missed. If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention? At that scale, the debate changes. It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.
tweets.content:
My two cents on rocket force debate :

Most commentators from the IAF are naturally looking at this through an Air Force lens, and that’s fair. Their concerns about duplication, joint planning, and command structures are valid.

But I think one important question is often missed.

If India’s ambition is to eventually field thousands of conventional ballistic, cruise, and hypersonic missiles, can missile warfare continue to be just another responsibility within the IAF, competing with fighters, AWACS, tankers, transport aircraft, and air defence for budgets and attention?

At that scale, the debate changes.

It isnt about IAF vs Rocket Force instead its question of whether missile warfare has become important enough to deserve its own doctrine, procurement priorities, and long-term institutional focus.

tweets.title: Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves. The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India. Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.
tweets.content:
Yes, no country can eliminate imports overnight. But every import should hurt our self-respect. We should feel ashamed that we still depend on others for critical technologies, and let that discomfort drive us to build them ourselves.

The goal should be simple: zero critical imports and as PM says an Atmanirbhar India.

Self-respect creates ambition. Ambition creates capability. That sense of self-respect must trickle from the new generation of leaders, who refuse to accept dependence as normal, to the older generation that grew up believing it was inevitable.

Defence Matrix (@Defencematrix1)

For country like India, Imports can't stop overnight since we were world's largest arms Importer for many decades, but a transition should come, where a roadmap is needed on where and how Imports should be done, Clarity on Imports should be there and companies which are actually trying to develop something locally on their own should also get rewarded. Screwdrivergiri also can't stop overnight but there should also be a clarity on the real nature of manufacturing. White labelling should be a crime till the system is completely localised, even in that case, till it is evolved with R&D for local requirement. Domestic name should not be given till it is domesticated and the issue is not just with private sector, it's equally with PSUs, in-fact PSUs survived on the very same thing, so taking this from private sector would be wrong, but the method should be corrected, hope this online discussion and debates should help in this cause.
Picture Credit @ajaishukla

The cycle goes like

Deploy -> learn -> refine -> redeploy

Chay Bowes (@BowesChay)

Germanys "Skynex batteries" were deployed to defend an industrial area in Ukraine, but failed dismally.

They included eight 35mm anti-aircraft installations, two radar stations, and two command posts. However, it all went very wrong very fast.

In the first minutes of the battle, three of the eight installations failed: the hydraulics malfunctioned, the tracking radar broke down, and the loading mechanism jammed. As a result, according to the Ukrainian side, only two installations were able to even track aerial targets.

The report's conclusions indicate that the Rheinmetall system demonstrated "low readiness for combat use" and its actual characteristics do not match those "claimed by the manufacturer"

Rheinmetall said any possible failures were attributed to "errors by Ukrainian crews"

The notifications cite their involvement in: - Cross-border infiltration into Jammu & Kashmir. - Recruitment, training, and financing of terrorists. - Arms and drone-based supply networks. - Major terror attacks, including the Nagrota (2016) and Sunjwan (2022) attacks This enables India to formally list these individuals as terrorists under domestic law.
tweets.content:

The notifications cite their involvement in:

- Cross-border infiltration into Jammu & Kashmir.
- Recruitment, training, and financing of terrorists.
- Arms and drone-based supply networks.
- Major terror attacks, including the Nagrota (2016) and Sunjwan (2022) attacks

This enables India to formally list these individuals as terrorists under domestic law.

tweets.title: There are no saints here : not even @ShivAroor bhai. 😂 and I understand his pressure. You just cannot ask them difficult questions else they won’t come (I tried it and I know - some of them in-fact asked questions in advance) I’d genuinely love to ask: what aircraft manufacturer anywhere in the world would happily accept an order split across three different production standards? 16 IOC fighters 16 FOC fighters 8 trainers Most OEMs would probably point to the exit. 😂and say that rectangular thing is door. That said, HAL isn’t beyond criticism either. Look at the timelines: 31 Mar 2006: Order for 16 IOC fighters + 4 trainers 23 Dec 2010: Order for 16 FOC fighters + 4 trainers 30 Sep 2014: First production IOC aircraft (SP-1) flies : 8 years after the first order 17 Mar 2020: First production FOC aircraft (SP-21) flies : nearly 10 years after the FOC order Then, after all that, the 83 Tejas Mk1A order received CCS approval only on 13 Jan 2021. The Tejas story has many villains. Procurement decisions, changing configurations, certification timelines, production bottlenecks, and industrial capacity all contributed. Blaming only the IAF or only HAL misses the bigger picture.
tweets.content:
There are no saints here : not even @ShivAroor bhai. 😂 and I understand his pressure. You just cannot ask them difficult questions else they won’t come (I tried it and I know - some of them in-fact asked questions in advance)

I’d genuinely love to ask: what aircraft manufacturer anywhere in the world would happily accept an order split across three different production standards?

16 IOC fighters
16 FOC fighters
8 trainers

Most OEMs would probably point to the exit. 😂and say that rectangular thing is door.

That said, HAL isn’t beyond criticism either. Look at the timelines:

31 Mar 2006: Order for 16 IOC fighters + 4 trainers
23 Dec 2010: Order for 16 FOC fighters + 4 trainers
30 Sep 2014: First production IOC aircraft (SP-1) flies : 8 years after the first order
17 Mar 2020: First production FOC aircraft (SP-21) flies : nearly 10 years after the FOC order

Then, after all that, the 83 Tejas Mk1A order received CCS approval only on 13 Jan 2021.

The Tejas story has many villains. Procurement decisions, changing configurations, certification timelines, production bottlenecks, and industrial capacity all contributed. Blaming only the IAF or only HAL misses the bigger picture.

tweets.title: LeT Reshuffles Leadership After Operation Sindoor Pakistan-based terror outfit Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) has reportedly carried out a major leadership reshuffle involving over 15 commanders across Pakistan's Punjab province following Operation Sindoor. According to open-source intelligence reports, the changes, ordered by Hafiz Saeed on 28 June, include the appointment of senior commanders to oversee training centres, recruitment networks, and infiltration routes. Key developments: • Haris Dar has been appointed to head Central Punjab, overseeing major LeT training facilities and infiltration corridors. • Imran Liaqat Bhatti, a senior arms trainer, has been moved to Kasur, which is reportedly emerging as a key training hub after the destruction of LeT's Muridke headquarters during Operation Sindoor. The reshuffle suggests LeT is attempting to reorganise and decentralise its infrastructure, dispersing training activities across multiple locations while reinforcing its command structure after recent operational setbacks.
tweets.content:
LeT Reshuffles Leadership After Operation Sindoor

Pakistan-based terror outfit Lashkar-e-Taiba (LeT) has reportedly carried out a major leadership reshuffle involving over 15 commanders across Pakistan's Punjab province following Operation Sindoor.

According to open-source intelligence reports, the changes, ordered by Hafiz Saeed on 28 June, include the appointment of senior commanders to oversee training centres, recruitment networks, and infiltration routes.

Key developments:
• Haris Dar has been appointed to head Central Punjab, overseeing major LeT training facilities and infiltration corridors.
• Imran Liaqat Bhatti, a senior arms trainer, has been moved to Kasur, which is reportedly emerging as a key training hub after the destruction of LeT's Muridke headquarters during Operation Sindoor.

The reshuffle suggests LeT is attempting to reorganise and decentralise its infrastructure, dispersing training activities across multiple locations while reinforcing its command structure after recent operational setbacks.

tweets.title: RT by @NetramDefence: INDUS WATERS TREATY: INDIA REJECTS HAGUE RULING India has rejected the Hague-based Court of Arbitration's ruling on the Indus Waters Treaty, maintaining that the court lacks jurisdiction because the treaty mandates a sequential dispute resolution process, starting with the Permanent Indus Commission, followed by a Neutral Expert, and only then arbitration. New Delhi also reiterated that the Indus Waters Treaty remains in abeyance following the Pahalgam terrorist attack and has asserted its right to fully utilize the waters allocated to India under the treaty. India continues to advocate a comprehensive review and modification of the 1960 treaty in light of current realities.
tweets.content:
INDUS WATERS TREATY: INDIA REJECTS HAGUE RULING

India has rejected the Hague-based Court of Arbitration's ruling on the Indus Waters Treaty, maintaining that the court lacks jurisdiction because the treaty mandates a sequential dispute resolution process, starting with the Permanent Indus Commission, followed by a Neutral Expert, and only then arbitration.

New Delhi also reiterated that the Indus Waters Treaty remains in abeyance following the Pahalgam terrorist attack and has asserted its right to fully utilize the waters allocated to India under the treaty.

India continues to advocate a comprehensive review and modification of the 1960 treaty in light of current realities.
